## [00:00 - 03:02]

Now that we've talked quite a bit about the YouTube platform for advertising, and now I want to go into a little bit more detail about how we're going to be able to target your customers, how we can get in front of them on YouTube. And really we're going to be using the platform of AdWords, which is part of Google's setup, and I'm sure you knew, but Google bought YouTube quite a few years ago, and they've integrated the whole system now so that YouTube works within AdWords and Google. And this means we can really take advantage of all the different targeting options that Google have had for a long, long time, and now are available for YouTube, and some of the possibilities on YouTube actually go further than what you would typically be able to do on a typical search or display campaign that you might run on Google. So what I'm going to do is walk you through some of the targeting options and just explain them, and if you look below this video we'll also kind of make sure it's nice and clear so you can kind of get your head around all the different targeting options, because later on when we start using them you'll be able to see why we use certain types of targeting to get started and what we use and what order and why we use them and for good reason. So let's talk it through, and there's eight in particular that I want to walk through. First of all, we've got keywords. Now, keywords is probably the easiest one to start with in terms of explaining how they work, because really what's happening is if someone types in a keyword into YouTube, then Google track that, and it means that we can then show our ads to people who have recently typed in that keyword. Now, the great thing about this is that time period will last for what Google called a session. So I've seen that they call a session on a mobile 40 minutes, whether it's the same on a computer or desktop, is unclear, but really it means that if you've gone to your computer and you've typed in a certain keyword that I want to advertise on, then it means that should you carry on watching videos for let's say the next 15 to 20 minutes or so, I can still run my ads to you because you recently typed in that keyword. But of course, AdWords works in such a way where it's tracking that data across Google and YouTube. So this also means that you can run your ads to people who have recently searched something on Google, and then have come to YouTube in the same session, and then you can run your ad to that person who recently typed in the keyword that you're targeting. And this really means that as you're advertising, you can say, right, if someone's typed in a keyword that's relevant to my business, in the last 15 to 20 minutes on Google or YouTube, I can run my ads to those people. It's a really effective way of running your campaigns and something definitely that's going to become integral to the way we build out your campaigns. You also have something called placements. Now placement targeting is where we can really pinpoint exactly what videos we want our ads to run on.

## [03:02 - 06:03]

So I'm sure you've seen things like pre-row ads on YouTube before where you get that skip ad button and you have to wait for five seconds before you can skip the ad. Those are pre-row ads or actually known as in-stream ads, and we'll talk about that shortly. With those ads running in front of other videos, as the advertiser, if you're advertising on YouTube, you can actually select which videos you want your ad to run in front of. You literally grab the URLs of the different videos you want your video ad to run in front of. And it means you can get in front of your audience based on which videos they're watching. And it gives you that real granular data that you might want to see, and it's a really effective type of targeting because you can say, right, if I want to get in front of that video because I know my audience will be definitely watching that video, you can choose to run your ad in front of that video, of course, if it's showing advertising, which is something we'll discuss later. So placement targeting is choosing different videos you want your ads to run in front of. Now, when it comes to the next type of targeting I want to show you, it's topic targeting. Now, topic targeting is where you can select what topics are videos you want to get your ads in front of. So let's say, for example, your audience tend to watch videos of a certain type of topic, and don't worry, these topics are actually laid out for you inside of the ad words interface. So you can literally just choose exactly what topics that seem most relevant to your business. And you can go down the list and say, okay, that one, that topic looks relevant, that topic looks relevant, and there's thousands of them. So you can really select exactly the right topic that seems to be most relevant for your business. And it means should anyone watch a video about that type of topic, then you can run your ad in front of that audience as well. Should you obviously be targeting and with that topic and paying to target that audience, then of course you can use topic targeting in your campaigns to get in front of your customers. So there's another form of almost like the search side of things, because obviously with placements and keywords and also topics, we're relying on people to be searching for information on YouTube and so we can get in front of them. Now you also have website remarketing. If you're doing a lot of Facebook advertising remarketing and retargeting, Facebook called it retargeting, Google called it remarketing, is pretty much exactly the same thing. So should someone visit your website, you can cookie them with a tag, and I'll talk about tags and pixels a lot later and how to install them correctly. That means if someone lands on your landing page, for example, and didn't opt in or take the next step, you can take that audience that went to your website and didn't sign up and run ads back in front of them. And if you've ever experienced that online, it can be done in a really effective way and improve the user experience, but I'm sure you've also had that experience where it feels like you go to a sales page.

## [06:03 - 09:08]

I've had it recently to go and buy a flight and I landed on like the British Airways page where they're talking about the flights to San Diego. But you do have website remarketing, that can be very, very powerful. You also have video remarketing. So should someone watch your video and not even visit your website, so they may have watched your video and then got distracted for some reason. You can take that audience that have watched your video and you can decide to follow up with more video ads to get back in front of that audience as well. Now these audiences are built inside of AdWords, and so you can build these people, these lists of people, you won't have their name and email or anything. Just be more people that have been cookied and will fall into a category of people that have watched your video ad. You can make a list of those people and decide to get back in front of them should you wish. So you can really have a much better user experience and almost create a story for them as opposed to just being bombarding people with the same ad over and over. Now Google, as I said earlier, are tracking all of our behavior, not just on the Google search page and on YouTube, but also it's got its kind of cookies all over the Internet. Google are tracking all of your movements as well. Now this means that Google can understand more about what people are in the market for. So if you've recently been, let's say for example, you've recently been like I was going to websites looking for flights to San Diego, or maybe looking for flights looking for car hire looking for hotels, Google can make a good indicating kind of can make a good guess to say, well, soon I'm likely to be going abroad or going on holiday soon or going to San Diego even. ## [09:08 - 12:13]

well, you're showing all the indications that you are looking for this type of thing. You're in the market for buying a TV, going on holiday, whatever it might be. They're tracking our behavior, what we're doing online, and then they'll put us into certain in market audiences. And this is perfect for us as advertisers because we can see exactly what those in market audiences are, and we can select the ones that seem to be most relevant to our business. Which means we can use all of Google's intelligence and use it for our advertising as well. You'll also have similar audiences, and this is again pulling on Google's intelligence. And if you're to have a lot of activity on your videos or on your website, or perhaps even if you upload your CRM, your email list to Google through something called Google Match, then if you were to upload that list or have those people hit some pixels on your website, for example, not only will Google say, great, well, we've built a list of those people for you, they'll also find other audiences that look just like the audiences you currently have. So let's say, for example, people are coming through your website opting in and hitting your first conversion pixel, so to speak. They've just opted in and they're at your site. Google will be able to take that list of people who have done that, and they'll know a lot of information about those people already. They'll be able to say to you, okay, well, based off those people who have already converted, here's an audience that are just like those people. Would you like to advertise to them as well? Now, on Facebook, you have an audience that's similar to this called a lookalike audience. Google do a very similar thing. It's built in a slightly different way. So on Facebook, it's like normally a 1% of the whole population of a country or 2% of the whole country. With Google, it's slightly different. They just say, hey, look, here's an audience that's very similar based on our data to that current audience that you have. Be it an email list or people who have taken some sort of action on your website, which makes it really easy to find more people who are just like your customers. And you can use these similar audiences to great effect as well. You also have affinity audiences. Now, these are somewhat similar to something like an in-market audience where people have recently been searching for information and they're in the market for something, but affinities are slightly different. So in market audience, it might be like there's a time limit to their interest in something. So if I was to be in the market for a holiday, I'm only going to be interested in that holiday for a couple of weeks, maybe. And so they're kind of like, they'll know that and I'll go into that audience and they might come back out of that audience on Google's data. Whereas with affinity audience, that's an audience that where someone has a long standing interest in something. And again, they'll break all the affinity audiences down for you so you can see exactly what ones might be most relevant to your business. Likewise, when you're getting data into your AdWords account and you're seeing exactly what's happening on your website, Google will be able to provide some information about those audiences and probably recommend different affinity audiences to you.
